pattern: Paying k once buys the freedom to reorder arr into any permutation at all, and the cheapest way to line up two multisets element-by-element under an absolute-difference cost is always to sort both and pair them in order -- so the answer is just the better of leaving the order alone or paying to sort.
hints: If you never rearrange, the cost is fixed: the sum of the absolute differences at each original position.
If you do rearrange, it costs a flat k no matter how much you shuffle, so once you're paying it you should aim for the best possible pairing between arr's values and brr's values.

The best possible pairing under an absolute-difference cost is achieved by sorting both arrays ascending and matching them index by index -- so compute both the no-rearrange cost and (k plus the sorted-pairing cost), and return the smaller one.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, rearranging plus targeted adjustments wins
already equal but rearranging is free -- should still cost zero
a large k makes rearranging never worthwhile even though sorted pairing would be cheaper
reversed order where sorting perfectly aligns both arrays for free extra cost beyond k
extreme values at the bounds of the range

stress.description: random arrays and random k across the full constraint range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    arr = [rng.randint(-100000, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    brr = [rng.randint(-100000, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 20000000000)
    return [arr, brr, k]

def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 7)
    arr = [rng.randint(-10, 10) for _ in range(n)]
    brr = [rng.randint(-10, 10) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 15)
    return [arr, brr, k]
